text: COLORADOS (POINT), the exterior E part of the harbour of Jagua in the island of Cuba, the entrance to which is in N lat. 22° 1′ 10", W long. 80° 36′ 35". From this point, a bank extends to the NW with 2 to 5 fath. water, on a rocky bottom, on which, in 1780, the frigate Santa Marta was lost, at the distance of 840 fath. from the coast. COL
COLORNO, a town of the duchy of Parma, 9 m. N of Parma, on the r. bank of the Parma. Pop. 2,000. There is a fine ducal palace here, with extensive gardens.
COLOUR, a town of Hindostan, in the Carnatic, 38 m. W of Nellore, on the r. bank of the Pennar, in N lat. 14° 31'.
COLOURI. See KOLURI.
COLPE, a parish in co. Meath, 21 m. SE of Drogheda. Area 5,785 acres. Pop. 2,141. It contains the villages of C., Betaghstown, Beamore, Donecarney, and Mornington.
COLQUEMARCA, a town of Peru, in the intendancy and 63 m. SSW of Cuzco, district of Paruco, between two of the head-streams of the Apurimac.
COLROY-LA-GRANDE, a commune of France, in the dep. of the Vosges, cant. of Saales. Pop. 1,278.
COLSTERWORTH, a parish of Lincolnshire, 34 m. S of Lincoln, on the Great North road. Area 3,000 acres. Pop. 1,017. It is noted as the birthplace of Sir Isaac Newton.
COLSTON-BASSET, a parish of Nottinghamshire, 10 m. ESE of Nottingham, on the Smite, and near the North West railway. Area 2,530 acres. Pop. 403.
COLTISHALL, a parish of Norfolk, 8 m. NNE of Norwich, on the Bure. Area 1,060 acres. Pop. 897.

COLTON, a parish of Norfolk, 53 m. N of Wymondham, on the Bure. Area 1,020 acres. Pop. 282. -Also a parish of Staffordshire, 14 m. NNE of Rugely, N of the Trent. Area 3,870 acres. Pop. 672.—Also a township in the p. of Bolton-Percy, in the ainsty and 65 m. SW of the city of York, intersected by the York and Derby railroad. Area 1,150 acres. Pop. 142.
COLT'S NECK, a flourishing village of Shrewsbury township, Monmouth co., in the state of New Jersey, U. S., 40 m. E of Trenton. In 1840 it consisted of about 20 dwellings.
COLUMB (St. Major), a market-town and parish of Cornwall, 32 m. SW of Launceston. Area 11,680 acres. Pop. 3,146.
COLUMB (St. MINOR), a parish of Cornwall, 54 m. WSW of St. Columb Major. Area 5,520 acres. Pop. 1,681.

COLUMBIA, a county in the E part of the state of New York, U.S., comprising an area of 624 sq. m., bounded on the W by Hudson river. It is watered also by Kinderhook, Claverack, Copake, and Ancram or Roeliff Jansen's creeks, all of which afford good water power, and intersected by the Hudson and Berkshire railroad. Towards the E it is hilly, and the soil indifferent; but in some parts it is fertile and highly cultivated. It contains iron ore, lead, oxide of manganese, marble, peat, and marl, and numerous thermal springs. Pop. in 1830, 38,325; in 1840, 43,252. Its capital is Hudson.—Also a county of the central part of the state of Pennsylvania, comprising an area of 700 sq. m., intersected by the E branch of the Susquehanna river, and Fishing, Catawissa, Big Roaring, and other creeks. It presents an irregular surface, but is generally fertile. Pop. in 1830, 20,049; in 1840, 24,267. Its capital is Danville.—Also a county in the E part of the state of Georgia, comprising an area of 600 sq. m., bounded on the NE by the Savannah. It possesses a finely undulating and fertile surface. Pop. in 1830, 12,606; in 1840, 11,356. Its capital is Applington.—Also a county in the N part of the state of Florida, containing a superficies of 4,320 sq. m., bounded on the W of Suwanne river, and watered also by St. Mary's river, Gadsden's spring, celebrated for its medicinal properties, and Randolph lake, which discharges itself into St. Mary's river. Except in the NE its soil is poor, and covered with pines. Pop. in 1840, 2,102. Its capital is Lancaster.—Also a township of Washington co., in the state of Maine, 15 m. W of Machias. Pop. 848.—Also a township of Coos co., in the state of New Hampshire, 126 m. N of Concord, bordered on the W by the Connecticut, and intersected by several small streams. It is generally mountainous. Pop. 620.—Also a township of Tolland co., in the state of Connecticut, 24 m. SE of Hartford, drained by a branch of Willimantie river, which affords good water power. Its surface is undulating, and its soil gravelly, but producing excellent pasturage. Pop. 842.—Also a township of Herkimer co., in the state of New York, drained by branches of Unadilla and Susquehanna rivers, and other streams which flow N into the Mohawk. It is hilly, and its soil consists of calcareous and sandy loam. Pop. 2,129.—Also a town of West Hempfield township, Lancaster co., in the state of Pennsylvania, 30 m. SE of Harrisburg, on the E bank of the Susquehanna river, which is here crossed by a bridge 5,690 ft. in length. It is connected by the Susquehanna canal with Havre-de- Grace, and intersected by the Columbia and Philadelphia railroad. Its trade with Philadelphia and Baltimore, in lumber, coal, iron, and produce, is considerable. Pop. 2,719.—Also a township of Bradford co., in the same state. Pop. 1,421.—Also a township of Jackson co., in the state of Michigan, 74 m. W of Detroit. Pop. 952.—Also a village of Fluvanna co., in the state of Virginia, 50 m. WNW of Richmond, at the confluence of James and Rivanna rivers. Pop. 200, of whom half are coloured.—Also a village of Tyrrell co., in the state of North Carolina, 200 m. E of Raleigh, on the E side of Scoupernony creek, a little E of its entrance into Albemarle sound.-Also a village of Richmond district, cap. of the state of South Carolina, 35 m. S of Camden, 120 m. NNW of Charleston, and 506 m. from Washington, in N lat. 33° 57', and W long. 81° 7'. It is situated in a plain on the E side of the Congaree river, immediately below the junction of Broad and Saluda rivers, and is connected with Charleston by a railroad. It is regularly laid out, and contains a considerable number of handsome houses, substantially built of brick. The greater number, however, are constructed of wood. It is well supplied with good spring water. Pop. 3,500.—Also a village of Marion co., in the state of Mississippi, 113 m. SSE of Jackson, on the E bank of Pearl river.—Also a village of Chicot co., in the state of Arkansas, 142 m. SE of Little Rock, on the W side of the Mississippi.—Also a village of Maury co., in the state of Tennessee, 42 m. SSW of Nashville, on the S bank of Duck river, the seat of Jackson college. Pop. 1,200.—Also a village of Whitley co., in the state of Indiana, on the NW side of Blue river.—Also a village of Adair co., in the state of Kentucky, 98 m. SW of Frankfort, on Russell's creek, a branch of Green river. Pop. 486. -Also a township of Boone co., in the state of Missouri, 36 m. N of Jefferson city, on Roche Pierre river, a branch of the Missouri. Pop. 3,365.—Also a village of Caldwell parish, in the state of Louisiana, 273 m. NW of New Orleans, on the W side of Washita river.—Also a township of Hamilton co., in the state of Ohio, 6 m. E of Cincinnati, bounded by Little Miami river on the SE, and Ohio river on the SW, and generally fertile. Pop. 3,043.--Also a township of Lorain co., in the E part of the state of Ohio, on both sides of Rocky river, 12 m. SE of Elyria, and 20 m. SW of Cleveland. Pop. 876.-Also a township of Gibson co., in the same state. Pop. 852.- Also a township of Meigs co., in the NW part of the same state, drained by the head waters of Leading creek, an affluent of the Ohio. Pop. 672.-Also a township of Randolph co., in the state of Arkansas. Pop. 680.—Also a town of West Hempfield township, Lancaster co., in the state of Pennsylvania. Pop. 2,716.

COLUMBIA (DISTRICT OF), a tract originally of 100 sq. m. on both sides of the Potomac, in North America, and about 120 m. direct distance from its mouth, which formed part of the states of Virginia and Maryland, but was ceded in 1790, at the suggestion of General Washington, for the purpose of becoming the seat of government of the United States. It comprised the city of Washington, and the towns of Alexandria and Georgetown; and, with the view of securing the functionaries of the federal government from the local jurisdiction of any single state, or collision with its authorities, is under the immediate government of Congress. In 1800 the pop. of this district was 14,093; in 1810, 24,023; in 1820, 33,039; in 1830, 39,858; in 1840, 43,712, of whom 30,657 were whites, 8,361 free coloured people, and 4,694 slaves. The surface of the district is gently undulating, furnishing fine sites for its cities, and particularly for Washington and its public buildings. The soil is naturally sterile, except on the level banks of the Potomac; but it possesses a fine healthy climate, with a mean temp. of 55°. This district has become the centre of a considerable and active commerce, though it cannot at all compete with Baltimore, in its vicinity. Vessels of the largest class come up to Alexandria, 6 m. below Washington, where the Potomac is 1 m. wide, and from 30 to 50 ft. deep; and vessels of a larger size come up to the U. S. navy-yard, at the junction of the E branch with the Potomac, at Washington. A branch of the Chesapeake and Ohio canal terminates at Washington. The Alexandria canal, a continuation of the Chesapeake and Ohio canal, runs 74 m. to Alexandria. A considerable quantity of flour and other produce is sent down the Potomac. This trade, however, centres chiefly at Alexandria. The chief business of Washington city has relation to the accommodation of the national legislature, and of the different offices of the general government.-This district was until 1846 divided into two counties: the co. of Washington on the N side of the Potomac; and the co. of Alexandria on the S side. In the former, the laws of Maryland continue in force; in the latter, those of Virginia. The district has never been represented in the Congress of the U.S., which, however, makes what laws it pleases for both. Congress meets annually at Washington, on the first Monday in December, unless otherwise provided by law. The president of the U.S., and the other chief officers of the government, reside at Washington; and the supreme court of the U.S. commences its sittings here annually, on the 2d Monday in January. The city of Washington was laid out on a broad scale in 1791; but its magnificent plan remains to a considerable extent in a state of nature, only a small portion of it having been built upon. In 1800, the seat of government was removed from Philadelphia, and permanently fixed in this place. By an act of Congress in 1846, the city and co. of Alexandria, having an area of 36 sq. m., were retroceded to the state of Virginia. During the war with Great Britain in 1814, the capitol was partially, and most of the other public buildings entirely burned by the British; but they have been rebuilt with greater magnificence.-George-town, finely situated on the NE side of the Potomac, is divided from Washington by Rock creek, over which there are 2 bridges .- See WASHINGTON.

COLUMBIA, a large river of N America,—called, it is said, by the natives Oregon, and by the Spaniards Hecera, -which rises, according to Major Pitcher, in the dividing ridge of the Rocky mountains, within a few yards of the source of the S fork of the Athabasca, one of the head-waters of the Mackenzie, which flows into the Arctic ocean, in a cleft of the great dividing chain overhung by Mount Brown [alt. 16,000 ft.] and Mount Hooker [alt. 15,000 ft.]; and, after a course of about 800 m. flows into the Pacific ocean, between Point Adams on the S, and Cape Disappointment on the N, in N lat. 46° 19', W long. 123° 54'. The head-stream of the C. is the Canoe river, whose source has been above described. Near the parallel of 52°, it is joined by two other streams, one from the N, and the other from the S. The river thus formed, and considered as the Main C., takes its course nearly due S, between lofty mountains, being generally 500 yards wide, but in some places spreading out into broad lakes. In 48° 12' it receives the Flatbow or M'Gillivray's river, and a little farther S the Flathead or Clarke's river. It then turns W, and, passing through a ridge of mountains, forms what are called the Kettle falls, 21 ft. high; and, continuing in the same direction between the 48th and 49th parallels, it receives the Spokan from the S and the Okinagam from the N. Near the 46th parallel, it is joined by the Lewis, its great S branch. It then flows W, receiving the Walla-Walla, the Umatala, the Day's river, and the Falls river, all from the S, and pursuing a tortuous course broken by rapids and whirlpools till it reaches the Falls, in which it descends 37 ft. 8 in. in 1,200 yards. Four miles lower down are the Dalles, or great rapids, formed by the passage of the water between vast masses of rock; and 40 m. below these are the Cascades, 125 m. from the mouth of the river. Near the Cascades, the C. is joined by the Clackamis from the S; a little farther down by the Willamette; and 30 m. lower by the Cowlitz. The three great tributaries of this river are the Willamette or Multnomah; Lewis's river, or the Sahaptin; and Clarke's or Flathead river; all flowing into it on the SE side: the Multnomah 139 m. from its mouth, Lewis's river 413 m., and Clarke's 600 m. At the point of the junction of Lewis's river, the C. is 960 yards wide. It is navigable as far as the Cascades, and during its lowest stages for vessels not drawing more than 12 ft. water. The tides rise and fall above Vancouver, 80 m. from its mouth; and during freshes, the C. rises at Vancouver 19 ft. above low water mark. The passage of the C. through the Cascade range extends for 2 m., in which distance the river falls 40 ft., and consequently is impracticable for boats. Above the Cascades, after a portage of 21 m., the navigation is good for 40 m., when it is interrupted by the Dalles, or 'Long Narrows,' in which the river is compressed into a channel half-a-mile long, and only 300 ft. wide, between high basaltic rocks, and has a fall of 50 ft. in 2 m. Fremont says: "The chasm at this place has the appearance of having been rent through the basaltic strata." At the narrowest place, he found the breadth by measurement 58 yards, and the average height of the walls above the water 25 ft. Six m. higher up, it is again interrupted by falls, above which the boat-navigation continues good to the junction of Lewis's river. The entrance of the Columbia lies between breakers, which extend from Cape Disappointment to a point on the S shore over a sort of bar or extensive flat with only from 43 to 5 fath. of water over it at the deepest point, and sand-banks occupying the middle of the river for 25 m. upwards. The entrance to the river, and the egress out of it, are difficult to shipping at all seasons, and from October to April extremely dangerous. In the opinion of experienced navigators, it cannot at any season be entered by loaded vessels of 400 tons. Great danger arises from the cross tides, the heavy swell, and a strong under-current. Yet the mouth of the C. is the only harbour for ships on the whole | river. COL -630 coast from the bay of San-Francisco to the straits of Fuca,—a distance equal to that from the straits of Gibraltar to the straits of Dover. The westerly wind prevails on this coast, and the sea breaks on the bar with great violence. The valley of the C. as high as the Cascades is divided into high and low prairies. The latter are not suitable for cultivation on account of their being annually overflowed during the freshets, but they are well adapted for grazing lands. The soil of the upper prairie is light and gravelly, and well covered with pines, arbutus, oaks, ash, and maple. In passing up the river, several low islands occur which are of the same character as the lower prairie. In the middle section of the basin of the C., the country is volcanic and barren. Above the Flatbow, where the C. expands into a succession of lakes, the country is covered with dense forests of pine and spruce. [Wilkes.] The climate is much milder than in the same parallel on the Atlantic coast. At Fort Vancouver, in the lat. nearly of Montreal, the cattle graze on fresh-growing grass throughout the winter; and the river is obstructed by ice but a few days in the beginning of January. The C. river and its valley form by far the most interesting and important portion of Oregon, not only on account of the advantages of soil and climate, but also from this river being the only line of communication between the sea-coast and the interior. "No great river," says Fremont, "does, or can, take its rise below the Cascade and Sierra Nevada range; the distance to the sea is too short to admit of it. The rivers of San Francisco bay, which are the largest after the C., are local to that bay, and lateral to the coast, having their sources about on a line with the Dalles of the C., and running each in a valley of its own, between the Coast-range, and the Cascade and Sierra Nevada range. The C. is the only river which traverses the whole breadth of the country, breaking through all the ranges, and entering the sea. Drawing its waters from a section of 10 degrees of lat. in the Rocky mountains—which are collected into one stream by three main forks (Lewis's, Clark's, and the North fork, near the centre of the Oregon valley)—this great river thence proceeds by a single channel to the sea, while its three forks lead each to a pass in the mountains, which opens the way into the interior of the continent. This fact in relation to the rivers of this region gives an immense value to the C. Its mouth is the only inlet and outlet to and from the sea; its three forks lead to the passes in the mountains; it is therefore the only line of communication between the Pacific and the interior of North America; and all operations of war or commerce, of national or social intercourse, must be conducted upon it. This gives it a value beyond estimation, and would involve irreparable injury if lost. In this unity and concentration of its waters, the Pacific side of our continent differs entirely from the Atlantic side, where the waters of the Alleghany mountains are dispersed into many rivers, having their different entrances into the sea, and opening many lines of communication with the interior."

COLUMBIANA, a county in the E part of the state of Ohio, U. S., comprising an area of 750 sq. m., bounded on the SE by the Ohio, and watered also by Little Beaver river and its branches. Its central district is level and extremely fertile, and is intersected by the Sandy and Beaver canal. It abounds with common and water limestone, and contains iron and marl. The climate is generally healthy, and the water good. Pop. in 1830, 35,508; in 1840, 40,378. It contains a village of the same name. Pop. in 1840, 273.—Also a village of Shelby co., in the state of Alabama, 73 m. E of Tuscaloosa, 7 m. W of Coosa
COLUMBIAVILLE, a village of Stockport township, Columbia co., in the state of New York, U. S., half-a-mile E of Hudson river, at the junction of Claverack and Kinderhook creeks. Pop. in 1840, 600.
COLUMBO. See COLOMBO.

COLUMBRETES, a group of rocky islets in the Mediterranean, off the coast of Valencia in Spain, 30 m. ESE of Cape Oropesa, in Valencia, in N lat. 39° 53', and E long. 0° 44'. They are 14 in number. The largest Columbreta has a small but well sheltered anchorage in W winds, on the NE side, in from 5 to 12 fath. Dwarf olives, geraniums, prickly pears, myrtles, and brushwood cover the hills; and an immense number of snakes exist upon these islands.

COLUMBUS, a county in the S part of the state of North Carolina, comprising an area of 525 sq. m., generally level and partly marshy. Pop. in 1830, 4,141; in 1840, 3,941. Its cap. is Whitesville.—Also a township of Chenango co., in the state of New York, 98 m. W of Albany. Its surface is hilly, and its soil clay and sandy loam. Pop. in 1840, 1,561.— Also a village of Mansfield township, Burlington co., in the state of New Jersey, 12 m. SE of Trenton, consisting in 1840 of about 30 dwellings.-Also a city in Franklin co., in the state of Ohio, capital of the state, 139 m. SW of Cleveland, and 110 m. NE of Cincinnati, in N lat. 39° 47', W long. 83° 3', and 3° W long. from Washington. It is situated on the E bank of Scioto river, opposite Franklinton, with which it is connected by a bridge, and immediately below the confluence of Whetstone river. It is regularly and substantially built; the streets cross each other at right angles, and the principal is 120 ft. in width. It contains several churches, some of which are handsome edifices; the state-house, the government offices, the penitentiary, the bank, and the public asylums, are also worthy of note. A wharf 1,300 ft. in length has been erected along the margin of the river. Pop. in 1840, 6,048.—Also a township of St. Clair co., in the state of Michigan, 37 m. NE of Detroit. Pop. 155.—Also a city in Muscogee co., of which it is the capital, in the state of Georgia, 124 m. WSW of Milledgeville, on the E bank of the Chattahoochee—which is here crossed by a bridge immediately below the falls-300 m. above the junction of Flint river, and 430 m. above Appalachicola bay. The city stands at an elevation of 60 ft. above the ordinary level of the river, and covers an area of 1,200 acres. The streets are spacious, and intersect each other at right angles. Some of the public buildings are very handsome. Regular steam-boat communication is carried on between this place and New Orleans. Pop. 3,114.—Also a city of Lowndes co., of which it is the capital, in the state of Mississippi, 141 m. NE of Jackson, on the E bank of the Tombigbee, 120 ft. above the level of the river, and at the head of steam-boat navigation. Pop. 4,000.—Also a village of Ballard co., in the state of Kentucky, 110 m. WSW of Frankfort, on the E bank of the Mississippi, 25 m. below the confluence of the Ohio. -Also a village of Bartholomew co., in the state of Indiana, 41 m. SSE of Indianapolis, on the E side of the East fork of White river. Pop. 2,438.—Also a township of Warren co., in the state of Pennsylvania. Pop. 1,169.—Also a village of Mansfield township, Burlington co., in the state of New Jersey, 12 m. SE of Trenton, consisting in 1840 of about 30 dwellings.

COLUMBUS (FORT), a military post of the United States, on New York harbour, in N lat. 40° 42', W long. 74° 9', at an alt. of 22 ft. above sea-level. The mean annual temp. at this point, on an average of 17 years, is 51° 82'. The average temp. of summer, 72° 08'; of winter, 31° 31'
COLUMPE. See PANOMPENG. COM
COLUNGODE, a town of Hindostan, in the SE division of the prov. of Malabar, 33 m. SE of Cherpool-Cherri, on an affluent of the Ponany.
COLVEND-AND-SOUTHWICK, a parish of Kirkcudbrightshire, extending N from the sea nearly 9 m., and about 7 m. in breadth. Pop. 1,495.
COLVESTON, a parish of Norfolk, 61 m. NNE of Brandon, and N of the Wissey or Stoke. Area 1,100 acres. Pop. 42.

COLVILLE, a large river in Arctic America, flowing into Harrison's bay, in about N lat. 70° 25', W long. 150° 35'. It is 2 m. broad at its mouth, and freshens the water for many miles. The Russian Fur company have a port on this river, which receives its supplies from Norton sound.—Also a small harbour and settlement on the SE point of Prince Edward's island.

COLVILLE (CAPE), or Moe-hao, the extreme headland of an extensive promontory, on the NE coast of the North island of New Zealand, on the E side of Shouraki gulf, or the frith of Thames, in S lat. 36° 36′, and E long. 175° 20′. The peninsula, consisting of trap rocks, rising into a range, whose mean alt. is 4,000 ft., and whose sides are thickly covered with wood, has a stern and imposing aspect.

COLVILLE (FORT), a settlement in Oregon, on the l. bank of the Columbia, in N lat. 48° 40′, 25 m. below the junction of Clarke's river. It occupies an embosomed position, in a woody, hilly country. Wheat and Indian corn are successfully cultivated here. From C. to the junction of the Spokane river with the Columbia, a distance of 60 m., the country is thinly covered with pine and spruce timber.
COLWALL, a parish of Herefordshire, 33 m. NNE of Ledbury, near the Malvern hills. Area 3,800 acres. Pop. 940.
COLWELL-AND-SWINBURN, a township in the p. of Chollerton, Northumberland, 8 m. NE of Hexham, E of the Tyne. Pop. 372.
COLWICK, a parish of Nottinghamshire, 21 m. E of Nottingham, on the N bank of the Trent. Area 1,010 acres. Pop. 109.
COLWICK, a parish and township of Staffordshire, 3 m. NNW of Rugeley, on the Trent, and intersected by the Grand Trunk canal. Area 8,800 acres. Pop. 2,024. Pop. of township, 205.
COLWINSTONE, a parish of Glamorganshire, 4 m. W of Cowbridge. Pop. 287.
COLWORTH, a tything in the p. of Oving, Sussex. Pop. 128.
COLWYN, a township in the p. of Llandrillo-yn- Rhôs, Denbighshire. Pop. 180.
COLY, a commune and town of France, in the dep. of Dordogne, cant. of Terasson, 22 m. from Sarlat. Pop. 278. Lithographic stone is quarried in the environs of the latter.
COLYNSPLAAT, a village of Holland, in the prov. of Zeeland, on the N coast of the island of North-Beveland, 14 m. NE of Middleburg.
COLYTON, a parish of Devon, 22 m. E of Exeter, on the Coly, near its confluence with the Axe. Area 5,430 acres. Pop., inclusive of the tything of Colyford, 2,451.
COLYTON-RAWLEIGH, a parish of Devon, 3 m. W of Sidmouth, on the Otter. Area 1,190 acres. Pop. 841.

COMACCHIO, a town of the Papal States, in the legation and 30 m. ESE of Ferrara, and 3 m. from the Adriatic. Pop. 5,500. It occupies an insalubrious situation in the midst of lagunes, called the Valdi-Comacchio. It was taken by the Imperialists in 1708, but was restored by Charles VI. in 1725 to Pope Benoit XIII. The lagunes lie between the Volano and Primaro, arms of the Po, and are intersected by numerous canals. They abound with fish, especially ~ eels, which, with the produce of the saline works, I constitute the only articles of local trade.
COMADERRY, a mountain in co. Wicklow, 3 m. W of Glendalough, between Glenanasane and Glenmalure. Alt. 1,567 ft. above the level of the Glendalough lakes; 2,268 ft. above sea-level.
COMADES, a group of 5 islets in the China sea, off the NW coast of the island Borneo, to the N of Point Barram, in N lat. 4° 55', and E long. 113° 30'.

COMANCHES, a numerous and warlike tribe of Indians in Mexico and Texas, whose principal haunts are in the prairie country beyond the rivers Del Norte and Pecos. At certain seasons of the year they undertake regularly organised expeditions into the states of Durango and Chihuahua. So regular are these expeditions that, in the Comanche calendar, the month of September is known as 'the Mexico moon,' as the other months are designated 'the buffalo moon,' 'the young bear moon,' 'the corn moon,' &c. They generally invade the country in three different divisions, of from 200 to 500 warriors in each. One, the most southern, passes the Rio Grande between the old presidios of San Juan and the mouth of the Pecos, and harries the fertile plains and wealthy haciendas of the Valle-de-San-Bartolomo, the Rio-Florida, San- José-del-Parral, and the Rio-Nasas. Every year the incursions of this band extend farther into the interior, as the frontier haciendas become depopulated by their ravages, and the villages are deserted. "For days together, in the Bolson-de-Mapimi," says Rushton, "I traversed a country completely deserted on this account, passing through ruined villages untrodden for years by foot of man." The central division enters between the Presidiodel-Norte and Mondova, where it is joined by the party coming in from the N; and passing the mountains of Mapimi, and traversing a desert country destitute of water, where they often suffer the greatest privations, they ravage the valleys of Mapimi, Guajoquila, and Chihuahua, and even the haciendas up to the foot of the Sierra Madre. "It appears incredible," adds Rushton, "that no steps are taken to protect the country from this invasion, which does not take the inhabitants on a sudden or unawares, but at certain and regular seasons, and from known points. Troops are certainly employed nominally to check the Indians, but very rarely attack them, although the Comanches give every opportunity, and, thoroughly despising them, meet them on the open field, and with equal numbers almost invariably defeat them." The C. claim to be descended from the subjects of Montezuma II., who, on the subjugation of the country by the Spaniards, preferring exile to servitude, set out towards the N, and, crossing the Rio-Grande, found a 'happy hunting-ground' [Tehas], which they decided should henceforth be their home.
COMANITZOVO, a town of Turkey in Europe, in Macedonia, in the sanjak and 30 m. SE of Monastir, to the E, and on an affluent of the lake of Kastoria.
COMARA, a lake of Brazil, in the prov. of Para, div. of the Rio Negro, which discharges itself into the Amazon, on the S side, in W long. 62° 10'.
COMARES, a town of Spain, in Andalusia, in the prov. and 13 m. N of Malaga, partido of Colmenar.

COMARGO, or Camarga, a town of Mexico, in the prov. of Tamaulipas, near the confluence of the Rio-San-Juan with the Rio-Bravo-del-Norte, and 70 m. WNW of the entrance of the latter into the gulf of Mexico.
COMAROS. See Gomaros.
COMAU, a town of Brazil, in Portuguese Guayana, on the W side of the estuary of the Amazon, at the mouth of the Motapi, 10 m. S of Fort Macapa.

COMAYAGUA, a town of Guatemala, cap. of the state of Honduras, on the l. bank of the Sirano, 100 m. N of the entrance of that river into the gulf of Conchagua, and 180 m. W of Guatemala. Pop. 18,000. It is the seat of a bishopric, and contains a fine church, a college, several convents, and a richly endowed hospital. It was founded in 1540, and originally called Nuestra-Senora-de-la-Conception, and subsequently New Valladolid.—The district of C. forms the S part of the prov. of Honduras, and contains 56,275 inha- 1,180 acres. Pop. 116. bitants. See Honduras.

COMBA, a town of Senegambia, in the territory of Kaarta, 45 m. S of Wassiboo, and 100 m. SE of Benowm.—Also a river of Senegambia, which descends from the mountains which form the W side of the upper bed of the Gambia, runs W, and unites with the Rio-Grande, in the district of Tenda Maie.
COMBA, an island of the Asiatic archipelago, in COM the Flores sea, NNE of the island Adenara, in S lat. 7° 45′, and E long. 123° 40′.

COMBA (BOELE), a Dutch settlement and garrison on the S coast of the SW arm of Celebes, near the W entrance of the straits of Salayer, and at the mouth of a considerable river, which has a bar nearly dry at low water, but with sufficient water for any boat at half-tide. There is good anchorage about 1 m. off shore in 11 fath.; and ships in want of water and refreshments can easily procure them here.

COMBACONUM, or Combooconum, a town of Hindostan, in the prov. of the Carnatic, district and 20 m. NE of Tanjore, and 35 m. ESE of Tranquebar, in the delta of the Caveri. The pop., consisting chiefly of Brahmins, is estimated at 40,000. It contains numerous tanks and pagodas; some of the latter are handsome edifices, but chiefly remarkable for the size of their outer gates, which invariably exceed the dimensions of the pagoda. The houses are neatly built, and the surrounding district fertile and wellcultivated. In the vicinity is a consecrated pond, celebrated for its healing virtues, and which, being supposed to be connected with the Ganges, periodically draws together an immense concourse of devotees.—This town was formerly the cap. of the Cholas, -one of the most ancient of the dynasties of Southern Hindostan, of whom any trace is to be found, and from whom the Cholamundel or Coromandel coast subsequently derived its name. Considerable remains of its former importance still exist in the locality.
COMBADAO (SAN), a town of Portugal, in the prov. of Beira, comarca of Arganil, 20 m. SW of Viseu, and 33 m. NE of Coimbra. It contains about 176 houses.

COMBADE, a river of France, which takes its rise in the dep. of Corrèze, in the com. of L'Egliseaux-Bois, and falls into the I. bank of Vienne, in the dep. of the Haute Vienne, at St.-Denis-les-Murs, after a course of about 24 m.
COMBADO, an islet of the Asiatic archipelago, in the Flores sea, between the islands of Butan and Token Besseys, in S lat. 5° 45', and E long. 123° 32'.
COMBANSON, a town of Lower Guinea, in Congo, 191 m. NE of St. Salvador.
COMBAPATA, a town of Peru, in the intendancy and 50 m. SSE of Cuzco, prov. and 5 m. NE of Tinta, on a small river of the same name, which flows into the Urubamba.
COMBARROS, a town of Spain, in the prov. and 28 m. WSW of Leon, partido of Astorga, on an affluent of the Tuerto.
COMBE, a parish in Hants, 63 m. ESE of Great Bedwin. Area 2,190 acres. Pop., including the hamlet of East Wick, 129.
COMBE-ABBAS, or ABBAS and TEMPLE-COMBE, a parish in the co. of Somerset, 33 m. S of Wincanton. Area 2,020 acres. Pop. 461.
COMBE-FIELDS, or Combe-Abbey, an extraparochial liberty in the co. of Warwick, 53 m. E of Coventry, near the Oxford canal. Area 4,210 acres. Pop. 195.
COMBE-FLOREY, a parish in the co. of Somerset, 5 m. NE of Wiveliscombe. Area 1,500 acres. Pop. 304.
COMBE-HAY, a parish in the co. of Somerset, 31 m. SW of Bath, intersected by the Bath canal. Area
COMBE-LONG, a parish in Oxfordshire, 21 m. SW of Woodstock, on the river Evenlode. Area 1,450 acres. Pop. 605.

COMBE-MARTIN, a parish and market-town in the co. of Devon, 4 m. E of Ilfordcombe. Area 4.730 acres. Pop. of p. 1,399. The town has a picturesque situation in a deep valley encompassed with hills, except on the NW at Combe-Martin cove, which opens into the British channel, and where fishing smacks and light vessels can lie. The houses extend along the dale, for nearly 1 m. from the sea-shore, amid woods and ridges of rocks tufted with foliage down to the level of the sea. The Combe-Martin and Beer-Alston mines have long been celebrated for their argentiferous lead ores. It is stated that the produce of these mines was great in the reign of Edward I. and II. In 1813 they were again opened and worked for four years, producing only 208 tons of ore in that time. In 1837 they were again opened.
COMBE-MONCTON, a parish in the co. of Somerset, 21 m. SSE of Bath. Area 720 acres. Pop. 1,107. It is intersected by the Bath canal.

COMBE-DE-NERVAUX, a hamlet of Switzerland, in the Cant.-de-Vaud, district and 8 m. W of Granson, and 28 m. NNW of Lausanne, supposed to be the Ariorica of Antoninus. It contains some blastfurnaces. COMBE ST. NICHOLAS, a parish in the co. of Somerset, 21 m. NNW of Chard. Area 4,100 acres. Pop. 1,293.
COMBE-PYNE, a parish in the co. of Devon, 4 m. E by S of Colyton. Area 1,070 acres. Pop. 143.
COMBE-RAWLEIGH, a parish in the co. of Devon, 13 m. NNW of Honiton. Area 2,100 acres. Pop. 276.
COMBE-IN-TEIGNHEAD, or COMBINTINHEAD, a parish in the co. of Devon, 3 m. E of Newton- Bushell, at the mouth of the Teign. Area 2,000 acres. Pop. 425.

COMBEAUFONTAINE, a canton and commune of France, in the dep. of the Haute-Saone, arrond. of Vesoul. The cant. comprises 17 com. Pop. in 1831, 9,167; in 1841, 8,977. The village is situated at the junction of three great roads, 21 m. from Vesoul. Pop. 806.

COMBER, a parish and town in co. Down. Area of p. 17,419 acres. Pop. 9,022.—The town stands at the head of the W projection of Lough-Strangford, 3 m. SSW of Newtownardes. Pop. 1,964. Linenweaving, two distilleries, and a large bleaching-green, give employment to a large proportion of the inhabitants.
COMBERBACH, a township in the p. of Great Budworth, co.-palatine of Chester, 3 m. NNW of Northwich. Pop. 175.
COMBERTON, a parish in the co. of Cambridge, 5½ m. WSW of Cambridge, N of Bourn Brook. Area 1,804 acres. Pop. 260.
COMBERTON (GREAT), a parish in the co. of Worcester, 23 m. SSE of Pershore, E of the river Avon. Area 960 acres. Pop. 215.
COMBERTON (LITTLE), a parish in the co. of Worcester, 2½ m. SE of Pershore. Area 770 acres. Pop. 229.

COMBIERS, a commune of France, in the dep. of the Charente, cant. of La Valette, on the r. bank of the Lizonne, 21 m. SE of Angoulême. Pop. 638. It has several blast-furnaces and forges, and a cannon-foundry; and contains a rich mine of iron.

COMBIN (MONT), a mountain of the Pennine Alps, lying between the frontiers of the Sardinian prov. of Aosta, and the Swiss cant. of the Valais, SE of Martigny, in N lat. 45° 56', E long. 7° 19'. Alt. 14,125 ft. above sea-level.

COMBLES, a canton, commune, and village of France, in the dep. of Somme, arrond. of Peronne.— The cant., comprising 21 com., had a pop. of 14,199 in 1841.—The com., 6 m. NNW of Peronne, had a pop. of 1,677 in 1831.
COMBOOCONUM. See COMBACONUM.

COMBOTI, a village of Turkey in Albania, 6 m. SSE of Arta, on a river of the same name, which, descending from the W flanks of Mont Chelona [alt. ] lar steps. Fraser estimates the alt. of C. above the COM 6,312 ft.], flows W by S, along the N skirts of the ridge of Drimonari, to the v. of C., where it turns SSW, and flows towards the gulf of Arta.

COMBOURG, a canton, commune, and town of France, in the dep. of Ille-et-Vilaine, arrond. of Saint-Malo.—The cant., comprising 10 com., had a pop. of 14,133 in 1841.—The com. and town, 20 m. SSE of Saint-Malo, had a pop. of 4,847 in 1841. There are extensive tanneries here.—M. de Chateaubriand was born at the chateau of C.
COMBRE', a commune of France, in the dep. of Maine-et-Loire, cant. of Pouancé. Pop. 1,209.
COMBRET, a commune and village of France, in the dep. of Aveyron, cant. of Saint-Sernin, on the r. bank of the Drance, 16 m. SW of Saint-Afrique. Pop. 1,237.
COMBRIT, a commune of France, in the dep. of Finistère, cant. of Pont-l'Abbé. Pop. 1,617.
COMBRONDE, a canton, commune, and town of France, in the dep. of Puy-de-Dôme, arrond. of Riom.—The cant., comprising 12 com., had a pop. of 9,721 in 1841.—The com. is 6 m. N of Riom. Pop. 1,955.
COMBROOK, a chapelry in the p. of Kineton, co. of Warwick, 2 m. W of Kineton. Pop. 282.
COMBROSSOL, a commune and village of France, in the dep. of Corrèze, cant. of Meynac. Pop. 1,012.
COMBS, a parish in the co. of Suffolk, 13 m. S of Market-Stow. Area 3,060 acres. Pop. 1,064.
COMBWELL, a manor in the p. of Goudhurst, co. of Kent, 21 m. S from Goudhurst.
COME. See Como.
COMERCOLLY, a town of Hindostan, in the prov. of Bengal, 64 m. SE of Murshedabad, near the Puddah branch of the Ganges.

COMET, a stream in NE Australia, which, coming from the SW, flows into Mackenzie river, on the r. bank, in about 23° 30' S. It was discovered by Dr. Leichardt, in December 1844, at which period its bed was nearly dry.
COMET ISLAND, a small island near the W coast of Northumberland inlet, in N lat. 65° 15'.

COMFORT (CAPE), a projecting bluff point on the NE coast of Southampton island, in Baffin's strait, in N lat. 65°, W long. 85° 20' [Baffin], N lat. 65° 54', W long. 82° 57' [Parry]. It rises nearly 1,000 ft. above the sea.

COMFORT (Point), a low, sandy point on the coast of Virginia, U.S., which juts out in a direction nearly S, at the junction of the Chesapeake bay on the E, with Hampton roads on the W, at the extreme end of the neck of land which separates James's river from York river. It is strongly fortified, with works, known as Fort Monroe, which cover an area of 60 acres, and is joined to the mainland by a narrow sandy neck, which is protected by a formidable redoubt thrown forward from the NW bastion of the principal fort. At the distance of 1 m. nearly due S from the point, on the other side of the entrance to Hampton roads, a powerful battery has been erected on an artificial island formed upon a mud bank on which there were formerly 17 ft. of water. The geog. position of Fort Monroe is N lat. 37° 2', W long. 76° 12'. The mean annual temp., calculated upon a series of 14 years' observations, is 59° 25'.

COMHARSEIN, a petty state of Northern India, chiefly comprised in one or two villages which stretch to the banks of the Sutledge, from the crest of the Whartu range at the foot of the Himalayas. The town, 10 m. S of Whartu, is a mere hill-village, built of dry stone and wood, in the Chinese fashion, but it commands one of the chief ghats to the S bank of the Sutledge, through a steep descent of 3 m. in length, of which the last mile is a continued flight of irreguriver at above 3,000 ft. This district is intersected by the parallel of 21° 23' N, and the meridian of 77° 20' E.
COMIAC, a commune of France, in the dep. of Lot, cant. of Bretenaux. Pop. 1,259.
COMILLAH, a town of Hindostan, in the prov. of Tipperah, in the presidency of Bengal, 50 m. SE of Dacca, on the Gomont.

COMINO, a small uninhabited island in the Mediterranean, lying between Malta and Gozo, in N lat. 36° 0′ 15", E long. 14° 19′ 40". It is 2 m. long from NE to SW, and about 1 m. broad.—On the SW is a rocky islet called Cominotto.

COMINO (CAPE), a promontory on the E coast of the island of Sardinia, in N lat. 40° 31′ 40″, E long. 9° 49′ 30″. Monte Albo, a tabled mountain 2,317 ft. high, stands a little inland and to the NW of the cape. With a point 4 m. to the NW, Cape C. forms the small bay of Siniscola.

COMISO', a small town and circondario of Sicily, in the district of Modica, 4 m. from Vittoria, 10 m. NW of Modica. Pop. 7,000. A copious spring rises in the midst of the market-place, which Fazellus and Sir R. C. Hoare identify with the Fons Diana. Its stream, which is copious, flows through the lake of Camarina, and discharges itself into the sea under the walls of Camarina. This stream is probably the Hipparis of Pindar.

COMITE, a river of Louisiana, U.S., rising within the Mississippi frontier, and flowing S, through East Feliciana co., and East Baton Rouge parishes, to the Amite river, which it joins on the r. bank.
COMITI, a town of Sicily, in the prov. of Trapani, 9 m. NNE of Mazzara. Pop. of district 3,500.

COMITLAN, or COMITAN, a town of Mexico, in the state of Chiapa, on a river of the same name, an affluent of the Tabasco, 60 m. SE of Ciudad-de-las- Casas. It has a superb church, and a large Dominican convent. It is chiefly supported by the contraband trade with Guatemala.
COMMANA, a commune of France, in the dep. of Finistère, cant. of Sizun. Pop. 2,670.
COMMANDATUBA, a serra of Brazil, in the prov. of Bahia, SE of Itaraca, in N lat. 15° 22' 8".- Also a v. in the comarca of Dos-Ilheos, in the prov. of Bahia, 28 m. S of Sao-Jorge.
COMMANDERIE (LA), a hamlet of France, in the dep. of Eure, com. of Saint-Colombe. Pop. 500.
COMMENAILLES, a commune and village of France, in the dep. of Jura, cant. of Chaumergy. Pop. 1,171.

COMMENDA, a settlement on the coast of Guinea, in SW Africa, 8 m. E of Elmina. It is situated in the Warsaw country, and was once a place of trade in gold-dust and ivory, and frequented by the slavetraders for the purchase of canoes. Both the English and the Dutch had forts here; but they have long since been abandoned. The English fort stands on the W side of the Soosn river. On the opposite bank, about 500 yards from it, are the ruins of the Dutch fort, which was partially destroyed in 1780. A native town stands at the foot of each fort. The waters of the Soosn seldom break through its high bar, but spread into a shallow pestilential lagune. On the beach E & S, 1 m. from the rocky point of C., is the village of Akalaki.
COMMENTRY, a commune and village of France, in the dep. of Allier, cant. of Montmarault. Pop. 1,277. Coal is extensively wrought here.
COMMEQUIERS, a commune and village of France, in the dep. of Vendée, cant. of Saint-Gillessur-Vie, 10 m. N of Les Sables. Pop. 1,407.
COMMER, a commune and village of France, in the dep. of Mayenne, cant. and 4 m. S of Mayenne. Pop. 1,392. COM

COMMERCY, an arrondissement, canton, commune, and town of France, in the dep. of La Meuse. —The arrond. has an area of 195,055 hectares. Pop. in 1831, 84,548; in 1841, 88,208. It comprises the 7 cant. of C., Gondrecourt, Pierrefitte, Saint-Mihiel, Vaucouleurs, Vignuelles, and Void.—The cant., comprising 29 com., had a pop. in 1841 of 15,945.—The com. and town are on the l. bank of the Meuse, 20 m. E of Bar-le-Duc. It is the seat of a communal college, and conducts an active commerce in grain, wine, oil, rape-seed, cattle, and leather. It is celebrated for its manufacture of cakes called Madelaines. The chateau of the princes of Vaudemont at this place has been converted into cavalry barracks. Pop. in 1821, 3,623; in 1841, 4,761.

COMMINES, or Comines, a commune and town of Belgium, in the prov. of W Flanders, arrond. and 10 m. SE of Ypres, on the l. bank of the Lys, opposite the French town of the same name, with which it communicates by a draw-bridge. Pop. in 1835, 3,187. It has active manufactories of ribbons, thread, muslins, nankeens, and tobacco; and extensive bleacheries, dyeing works, and tanneries. Philippe de Comines was born at the chateau of C. about 1445.— Also a commune and village of France, in the dep. of Nord, arrond. and 8 m. N of Lille, on the r. bank of the Lys. Pop. in 1841, 5,161. It has considerable manufactories of ribbons, thread, and cottons, with some bleacheries and oil-works.

COMO, a delegation or administrative subdivision of Austrian Lombardy, in the gov. of Milan; bounded on the N by the Swiss cant. of Ticino; on the NE by the prov. of Valtellina; on the E by the prov. of Bergamo; on the S by that of Milan; and on the W by the Sardinian states, from which it is separated by Lago-Maggiore. It is of very irregular outline. Its length from E to W is about 48 m.; its breadth from N to S varies from 9 to 50 m. Its superficies is estimated at 1,097 sq. m., of which nearly fourfifths are productive surface. Pop. in 1827, 345,125; in 1837, 370,893. The Lepontine Alps cover the N districts, and form several beautiful valleys, such as the Val-Sassina, and the Val-Assina, in which various streams originate, among the most important of which are the Olona, the Seveso, the Lambro, and the Adda. The E division encloses the celebrated Lago-di-C.; the W comprises the E shores of Lago- Maggiore; on the W is the Lago-Varese; and on the SW is the Lago-di-Lugano, of which the NE extremity belongs to this prov. The principal productions of the soil are corn, wine, fruit, and silk. Iron occurs in some quarters, but is not wrought; alabaster, slate, and freestone, are extensively quarried. The pop. is industrious, and supplies a large proportion of the well-known itinerant venders of barometers, mirrors, spectacles, framed prints, and wares of like class, which are largely and cheaply manufactured at the town of C. The other manufactures consist of woollen and silk goods, fire-arms, paper, soap, and musical instrument strings.-The prov. is divided into 26 distretto, and 530 communi; and comprises 2 towns, 32 market-towns, and 939 villages. Como, the capital of the above deleg., is situated at the SW extremity of the Lago-di-Como, in N lat. 45° 48′ 20″, E long. 9° 5′ 46″, 22 m. NNW of Milan, at an alt. of 652 ft. above sea-level. It is built around the semicircular termination of the lake. An amphitheatre of low hills surrounds it at a little distance, and on one side rises a steep conical mountain, surmounted with the old fort of Baradello. It is surrounded by double walls, flanked with massive towers. C. is an episcopal city; and contains a large and curious cathedral, founded in 1396, of mixed and semibarbarous architecture, in which "the elegant Gothic is mingled with the grotesque forms of ruder orders." The interior of the town exhibits "dark, narrow, and filthy streets; churches numerous, old, and tawdry; some dreary palaces of the Comesque nobles; and dismantled dwellings of the cittadini." [Lady Morgan.] C. possesses a lyceum, a public library of 15,000 vols., and 3 gymnasia, a collection of natural history, and a botanical garden. It has some manufactures of soap, silk, and cotton. Its silk fabrics formerly stood next in rank to those of Milan; and the number of looms at work in C. once exceeded those of Lyons. [Bowring.] A considerable proportion of the pop. is engaged in the contraband trade between Switzerland and Lombardy. The pop. of C. in 1837 was 16,177.—About 3½ m. from C., on the W shore of the lake, is the Villa d'Este, for some time the property and residence of Queen Caroline of England. It was purchased by her from General Pino, and sold to her banker, and present possessor, the Duke Torlogna. In the suburb of Borgo-de-Vico are the Odescalchi and Jovian palaces.—C. was the Comum of the Romans. In the Middle ages it was the capital of a small republic of the Ghibelline party, and was taken and burnt by the Milanese in 1127, and again in 1271. It was incorporated with the duchy of Milan in 1335. Under the French, it was the cap. of the dep. of Lario.-C. was the birth-place of the younger Pliny, of Paulus Jovius, and the popes Clement and Innocent XI.

COMO (LAGO-DI), a beautiful sheet of water lying between two ridges of the Rhætian Alps, in Austrian Lombardy, in the deleg. of Como, the Cumanus lacus or Comensis lacus, of the Middle ages, and the Lacus Larius of the Romans. It is intersected by the parallel of 46° N; and is reckoned about 42 m. in length from N to S, or from Chiavenna to the town of Como, and from 1 to 41 m. in breadth. Its greatest breadth occurs between Menagio and Varena. It is of very irregular figure, consisting of three long narrow arms, one stretching N, another S by E, and another S by W. Its area is estimated at 61 sq. m. Its N arm, formed by the waters which descend from the Splugen, by the Val-San-Giacomo, is called the Lagherro, or the Lago-di-Chiavenna, or di Riva, from the town of that name, the port of Chiavenna. The navigation of this lake is dangerous, owing to shallows, which prevent the Como steam-boats from ascending above Gravedona; but boats may be obtained at Riva, by which travellers can descend the lake of Como. The marshy shores of the lake of Riva, as well as the mouth of the Adda, are infested with malaria to a fatal extent; and no time should be lost, by those who take the Splugen road, in hastening through this part of the route. The Adda pours the waters of the Val-Teline into the lake, on the E side, nearly opposite to Gravedona, at the head of what may be properly called the lake of Como, a little below the channel which connects it with the Laghetto or Upper lake. From this point, its waters spread in an unbroken S course for 15 m., as far as the point of Bellagio, by which the lake, when about 3 m. wide, is divided, in an acute angle, into two branches. The wider and larger branch, which is about 18 m. in length, and has no outlet, extends in a SW direction to the town of Como, and retains the name of the LAGO-DI-COMO. The view of the lake from this town is confined to a small circular basin surrounded by high hills, and enlivened by villas; but on doubling the low headland of Cernobio a very beautiful reach opens up, in which the mountains rise on each side boldly from the water's edge, their ascent covered by a triple plantation of olives, vines, and chestnut trees, and their summits terminating in peaks of varied form and elevation. The SE branch, 12 m. in length, is called the Lago-

DI-LECCO, from the town of that name, near which it begins gradually to narrow into the Adda. Owing COM to the want of an outlet at Como, the waters of that branch are forced to return to the Point of Bellagio, and are thus subjected to constant interruption, either in their downward or upward course, by the cross influence of the wind; so that one side is frequently excessively agitated, while the other is perfectly calm. From this circumstance, and from the vast height of the bordering mountains, the navigation is rendered uncertain and sometimes dangerous by the violent swells and squalls to which it is particularly liable. Steam-boats, however, navigate the lake from one extremity to the other in 4 or 5 hours. The depth, according to Eustace, varies from 40 to 600 ft. It deepens rapidly from the edge. In summer the water is higher than at other seasons from the melting of the snows. "Throughout its whole extent, the banks of the lake are formed of precipitous mountains from 2,000 to 3,000 ft. in height; in some places overhanging the water, in others partially clothed with wood, and studded with hamlets, cottages, villas, chapels, and convents. But a vast extent of the scenery is bare; for the woods, luxuriant and beautiful as they are on the immediate shores of the lake, bear but little proportion to the bordering mountains, where the crags and cliffs, partly from their excessive steepness, partly from the dryness of the soil and the burning effects of Italian suns, nourish no vegetable production whatever, but present an aspect of glaring, arid whiteness. This defect prevails throughout the greater part of Alpine and Apennine scenery, and is particularly striking on the Lakes of Como and Lugano. The most beautiful point of view in the whole extent is undoubtedly at Bellagio." [Sketches of Italy, vol. iv.] From Chiavenna, at the head of the lake, there is a footpath over the Splugen, descending along the upper valley of the Rhine to Coire. From Menagio, a village 3 m. above Cadenabbia, on the W shore, a narrow road leads over the mountains to Portezza, on the Lago-di-Lugano. —The alt. of this lake above sea-level is 649 ft., which is said to be 200 ft. below that of the Lago-di- Lugano to the W of it; and 393 ft. above that of the Lago-di-Garda on the E.

COMODO, an island in the Timor group, forming the E side of Sapy strait, in the Eastern sea, opposite the SE end of Sambava, in S lat. 8° 38', E long. 119° 45'. It is a high rocky mass, covered with wood. To the E of it, is the strait of Mangerye, by which it is separated from Flores.

COMORES, or Comoro Islands, a group of four high islands at the N end of the Mozambique channel. The distance of the nearest island, Mohilla, from Mozambique, is about 250 m.; and from thence to Johanna, the principal island of the group, and the residence of the Sultan, is about 30 m. Johanna or Anzuan lies nearly in the middle of the channel between Madagascar and the African mainland, just where it widens into the open sea. See ANZUAN. C. island, the northernmost of the group, is about 90 m. from Johanna, in S lat. 11° 32', E long. 43° 25'. Its mountains are supposed to attain an alt. of 6,000 ft. Its highest peak is in S lat. 12° 15', E long. 44° 35'. Mayotta, the easternmost island, has its S extremity in S lat. 13° 5'. The inhabitants of these islands are principally of Moorish origin, and nearly all Mahommedans. They speak Arabic, and wear the turban and loose dress, which are seen on no part of the neighbouring coast. They live chiefly on vegetables and milk. Previously to Britain possessing Cape town and St. Helena, English ships were in the habit of touching at these islands for refreshments on their voyages to and from India. American whalers also frequently touch at these islands. The French obtained the cession of Mayotta on the 13th of June 1843; and have transported the condemned insurgents of June 1849 to this island, to be employed on extensive fortifications now in progress upon it. See MAYOTTA.

COMORIN (CAPE), a low sandy circular point, forming the S extremity of the great Asiatic peninsula of Hindostan, in N lat. 8° 05', E long. 77° 44' [Horsburgh]; N lat. 8° 5', E long. 77° 30' [Raper]. It is sometimes described as forming the extreme S point of the great western or Malabar chain of India, which, commencing in Candeish, runs along the Malabar coast, within a short distance of the sea, and in an unbroken chain, except where interrupted between the parallels of 10° 40' and 11° by the great gap which opens into the valley of Coimbatore. But the extreme S part of the continent slopes with a very gentle declivity from the base of Komari, the S extremity of the Western range [alt. 3,882 ft.] to the sea, over a distance of from 1½ to 2 m.; and the actual point or cape is not discernible at above 31 or 4 leagues' distance, from the deck of a large ship. Within 2 or 3 cables' length of the SE part of the point, lies a high rocky islet, with other rocks about it on which the sea breaks.
COMORN. See Komorn.
COMPAINS, a village of France, in the dep. of the Puy-de-Dôme, cant. of Besse, on the Creuse, 27 m. S of Clermont-Ferrand. Pop. 856. In the environs is a mine of iron.
COMPANY'S. See URUP'S LAND.

COMPASS, or Spitzkop-Berg, a mountain of South Africa, in the prov. and 30 m. N of Graaf- Reynet. It rises to the height of 6,394 ft.; and forms the highest summit of the Sneeuwberg or Snow Mountains. The Zeekoe or Sea Cow river, an affluent of Nu Gariep river, descends from its northern, and the Cat river, a tributary of Sunday river, from its NW side.
COMPATSCH, a town of Switzerland, in the Unter-Engadin, cant. of the Grisons, on the l. bank of the Saumaun, 5 m. W of its confluence with the Inn.
COMPE, a town of Sudan, in the territory of Ludamer, 10 m. SE of Benown.
COMPETA, a town of Spain, in Andalusia, in the prov. and 24 m. ENE of Malaga, partido of Torrox.
COMPEYRE, a commune and town of France, in the dep. of Aveyron, cant. and 6 m. N of Millau, on the r. bank of the Tarn. Pop. 787.
COMPIANO, a town of the duchy of Parma, in the district and 32 m. SSW of Borgo-San-Donnino, on the l. bank of the Tarro. Pop. 1,800. It contains some blast furnaces and forges.

COMPIE'GNE, an arrondissement, canton, commune, and town of France, in the dep. of the Oise. —The arrond. comprises an area of 127,528 hectares, and contains 8 cant., viz.: Attichy, Compiègne, Estrees-Saint-Denis, Guiscard, Lassigny, Noyon, Ressons, and Ribecourt. Pop. in 1831, 98,812; in 1846, 98,807.—The cant., comprising 12 com, had in 1841 a pop. of 16,353.—The town occupies an agreeable situation on a rising ground on the l. bank of the Oise, 1½ m. below the confluence of the Aisne, 36 m. ESE of Beauvais, and 47 m. NNE of Paris, in N lat. 49° 25′ 3″. Pop. in 1789, 5,876; in 1836, 8,895; and, in 1846, 9,076. The streets are tortuous, and the houses, with the exception of those adjacent to the castle, badly built. The castle-which is a magnificent structure—was originally a royal huntingseat. The present edifice was erected during the reigns of Louis XIV., his two immediate successors, and Napoleon. Its apartments, especially the state bed-room, cabinet, and library, are of the most sumptuous description,-the former is considered the finest in France. The adjacent forest comprises an area of 15,000 hectares. The Abbey church of S. COM Corneille, founded by Charles the Bald, the churches of St. Antoine and St. Jacque, the Pont-Neuf of Louis XIV., which here crosses the Oise, and the town-house, a Gothic structure, are all handsome edifices. The public library, the college, and the theatre, are also worthy of notice. C. has considerable manufactories of linen, muslin, hosiery, cordage, and wooden-ware; and an active trade in grain, hemp, timber, and charcoal. Fairs are held monthly.-C., which was of Gaulish origin, was constituted a royal town by Clovis. In 876 it was to a great extent rebuilt by Charles the Bald, and named by him Carlopolis. It was the seat of several councils, of which one of the most noted was that in 833, in which Louisle-Débonnaire was deposed. In 1415 it was taken by Charles VI., and in 1430 it sustained a siege by the English, who here made Joan of Arc prisoner. The treaty of 1624 between France and Holland was negotiated here.

COMPOSTE'LLA (SANTIAGO-DE), a large and well-built town of Spain, the capital of Galicia, the seat of an archbishop's see, and of an university. It is situated on an eminence, near the confluence of the Noya and the Arosa, in N lat. 42° 52'; 300 m. NW of Madrid. The streets, with two exceptions, are narrow and ill-paved. The public squares and the churches, particularly the metropolitan church, are very magnificent. The latter is 300 ft. in length, and has 23 chapels. Its façade is striking only from its extent and crowded decoration. Its see is the richest in Spain; and its chapters are numerous and wealthy. It is pretended that the body of St. James the Elder, the tutelar saint of Spain, was buried here, -a circumstance which draws a great number of pilgrims from most parts of Christendom. The poor pilgrims are received into an hospital, built for that purpose, which stands near the church on the N side of the Great plaza. The university, founded in 1532, has four faculties, and a good library. It was attended by 1,030 students in 1845. All the minor colleges have been incorporated with it. From this town the military order of St. Jago, or St. James, had its origin. A traffic is carried on in wine, fruit, and fish; and there are also manufactures of silk stockings, hats, paper, and leather. The pop. is estimated at 28,500. The land in the vicinity is wellcultivated.—The name Compostella is derived from Campus stellæ; the legendary history of the place informing us that a star pointed out the spot of sepulture of the Saint's body.

COMPOSTELLA, a town of Mexico, in the state of Xalisco, founded in 1531. Tobacco of a superior quality was formerly cultivated in the adjacent country. The soil of the environs is barren; but there are several silver-mines in the neighbourhood.
COMPREIGNAC, a commune of France, in the dep. of the Haute-Vienne, cant. of Nantiat, in a mountainous locality, near the source of the Vincon, 15 m. SE of Bellac. Pop. 2,254.

COMPRIDA, one of the larger islands of the coast of Parati, in the prov. of Rio-de-Janeiro, Brazil, to the SW of the bay of Netherohi, and 21 m. NNE of the island of Redonda. There are two smaller islands of the same name in the same prov.

COMPRIDO, a small river of Brazil, in the prov. of Rio-de-Janeiro, which descends from the Serrade-Tejuco, and by means of a canal forms one of the chief sources of supply of water to the city of Rio-de- Janeiro.

COMPS, a canton and commune of France, in the dep. of the Var, arrond. of Draguignan .- The cant. comprises 10 com. Pop. in 1831, 3,683; in 1841, 3,501.—The com. is on the r. bank of the Artube, 10 m. N of Draguignan. Pop. 858.
COMPS-LA-GRANVELLE, a commune of France, in the dep. of Aveyron, cant. of Cassagnes- Begonhes. Pop. 340.

COMPTON, a parish in Berks, 2 m. E by S of East Isley. Area 4,050 acres. Pop. 544.—Also a tything in the p. of Newent, co. of Gloucester, 9 m. NW of Gloucester. Pop. 490.—Also a tything in the p. of Henbury, co. of Gloucester, 6 m. NNW of Bristol. - Pop. 144.—Also a parish in Hants, 21 m. SSW of Winchester, intersected by the Southampton and London railway. Area 1,800 acres. Pop. 304. -Also a parish in Surrey, 31 m. NNW of Guildford. Area 1,790 acres. Pop. 522.—Also a parish in Sussex, 8 m. SW of Midhurst. Area 1,750 acres. Pop. 274.
COMPTON-ABBAS, or West, a parish, including the tything of Bedhurst, co. of Dorset, 8 m. NNW of Dorchester. Area 2,170 acres. Pop. 91.
COMPTON-ABBAS, a parish in the co. of Dorset, 3 m. S of Shaftesbury. Area 1,330 acres. Pop. 439.
COMPTON-ABDALE, a parish in the co. of Gloucester, 3 m. NW by W of North Leach. Area 2,040 acres. Pop. 260.
COMPTON-BASSET, a parish in the co. of Wilts, 2 m. ENE of Calne. Area 1,980 acres. Pop. 498.
COMPTON-BEAUCHAMP, a parish in Berks, 51 m. S of Great Farringdon, in the vicinity of the Great Western railway. Area 1,890 acres. Pop., including the hamlet of Knighton, 157.
COMPTON-BISHOP, a parish in Somersetshire, 13 m. W of Axbridge, N of the river Axe. Area 2,510 acres. Pop. 802.
COMPTON-CHAMBERLAYNE, a parish in the co. of Wilts, 41 m. SW by S of Wilton. Area 2,130 acres. Pop. 350.
